critique.summary: The article discusses seven common mistakes to avoid when creating system architecture diagrams, emphasizing clarity and accuracy in representing system components and their relationships. It highlights issues such as unlabeled resources, disconnected elements, overly complex 'master' diagrams, oversimplified behavioral diagrams, distracting animations, fan traps, and overreliance on AI-generated diagrams.
mainPoints: Resources in diagrams should be labeled with both their type and specific names to avoid confusion.
All resources should be connected to others to clearly show their role and relationships within the system.
Creating a single 'master' diagram that attempts to show an entire system at once often overwhelms viewers and should be broken into multiple perspectives.
Behavioral diagrams should accurately represent interactions, avoiding oversimplification like the 'conveyor belt syndrome' that misleads viewers.
Meaningless animations in diagrams add no informational value and can distract from the technical content.
Fan traps occur when relationships between resources are lost due to collapsing relations onto intermediate resources, which can be fixed by adding more specific intermediate elements.
AI-generated system diagrams from source code are currently unreliable due to vague outputs, hallucinations, and lack of strategic inclusion of details.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides practical and accurate advice consistent with best practices in system architecture diagramming. The points about labeling, connectivity, diagram complexity, and the limitations of AI in generating diagrams align with common knowledge in software engineering and architecture documentation. The examples cited, such as issues with 'master' diagrams and fan traps, are well-recognized problems in diagramming. The caution against misleading simplifications and meaningless animations is also valid. However, the article is based primarily on the author's expertise and examples rather than citing multiple external sources or studies, which limits its journalistic robustness.
considerations: The article does not provide empirical data or references to industry standards or academic research to support its claims.
It focuses mainly on AWS-related examples, which may not fully represent all system architecture contexts.
The discussion on AI limitations could be expanded with references to current AI diagramming tools and their capabilities or shortcomings.
The article assumes a certain level of familiarity with system architecture concepts and diagram types, which might limit accessibility for novices.
No alternative viewpoints or counterarguments are presented regarding the use of animations or AI in diagramming.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around common mistakes and best practices in creating system architecture diagrams. Commenters emphasize clarity in arrow meanings, knowing the audience, and balancing detail versus simplicity, with some skepticism about the practical usefulness of complex diagrams.

comments.author: orthoxerox
comments.text:
The most common mistake I've seen is not agreeing on what arrows represent: control or data. Does A-(customer data)->B mean A asks B for data or A sends customer data to B?

Of course, sequence diagrams make it clear with two separate arrows when control and data flow in different directions, but a lot of diagrams are of the "plain old boxes and arrows" variety.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a fundamental ambiguity in diagrams that can cause misinterpretation, making it a valuable and accurate observation.

comments.author: icedchai
comments.text:
The biggest mistake is not knowing your audience.

Is the diagram for marketing? A sales proposal? A business person using the product? Technical peer?

If you don't know this, you don't know if you have the right level of detail.
comments.analysis: Knowing the audience is crucial for deciding the level of detail and style of diagrams, making this a key insight for effective communication.

comments.author: dematz
comments.text:
Idk, while system architecture diagrams look cool and feel informative, I generally don't feel like they actually help you get started working somewhere on a project. Mistake #3 in this article, putting too much in, is part of this.

So https://www.jerf.org/iri/post/2025/on_layers_and_boxes_and_l... is an interesting take: put links in your diagram, so it functions as a table of contents. This seems most useful for someone who needs to start working on a project.

Similarly https://haskellforall.com/2026/02/browse-code-by-meaning asks how to show what's in a repo, but maybe file tree is not best and a diagram with links as table of contents is the answer.

That said practically speaking, I'm not sure what tooling easily creates working links in a diagram that looks good in any context, for instance mermaid might render on github but not a text editor.

Of course for other purposes maybe just go crazy with the diagram. I once had a coworker draw this super detailed master diagram, maybe 50-100 things on it, which I was told impressed senior government officials (after my manager recolored all the red to avoid connoting errors). But for the purpose of orienting developers a table of contents with links sounds better.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical perspective on the limited utility of overly detailed diagrams for onboarding, suggesting linked diagrams as a better approach.

comments.author: datadrivenangel
comments.text: Diagrams are communication tools, and are best done with a target and goal in mind. The C4 framework is good for addressing multiple levels of abstraction and different types of viewers. The business execs don't need the level of detail that someone debugging the system does.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly emphasizes the importance of purpose-driven diagrams and references a well-known framework, adding credibility.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: The worst ones are diagrams that look clean but hide all the decisions that actually matter. A messy diagram that shows the real tradeoffs is more useful than a pretty one that lies
comments.analysis: This comment stresses the value of honesty and transparency in diagrams over superficial neatness, which is an important consideration.

comments.author: dawnerd
comments.text:
This is just an advertisement for their service.

In my 20 years in this field I can easily count on one hand the times a diagram like this has been useful. I’ve seen more cases where they were clearly created to satisfy some exec that wanted to see it and never updated again.
comments.analysis: While somewhat dismissive, this comment reflects a common skepticism about the practical usefulness of such diagrams, though it lacks constructive alternatives.

comments.author: rawgabbit
comments.text: I generally have given up on diagrams. Systems and flows I work with are too convoluted to be mapped out. Only the simplest of flows can be diagrammed and it usually leaves out important facts. When dealing with non technical people, I have found out through trial and error that excel works best. I start out with sample data on one sheet and walk them through the various transformations in sheet2, sheet3 etc. I even create a table of contents that has links to the different sheets. In a phrase, seeing data is believing.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic view on the limitations of diagrams for complex systems and suggests alternative approaches like using spreadsheets.

comments.author: zabzonk
comments.text:
Couple of comments:

> This can be as simple as adding a type suffix to a resource name (e.g. Orders Table, Results Bucket)

Don't encode types in names. And I disagree somewhat that the names are really needed at all.

> Making a “master” diagram

I think such a diagram is useful but obviously each top-level "box" in it doesn't need to contain all sub-components.
comments.analysis: This comment offers nuanced advice on naming conventions and diagram granularity, contributing useful design considerations.

comments.author: kingforaday
comments.text: Their master diagram example in #3 contains a #2 mistake with an unconnected resource (the stripe account). Maybe a double validation of why the master diagrams can be hard to maintain.
comments.analysis: This comment points out a specific example of a common diagram mistake, reinforcing the difficulty of maintaining comprehensive diagrams.

comments.author: ranman
comments.text: Route53 being off on the side but unconnected is still valuable info...
comments.analysis: This comment suggests that even unconnected elements in diagrams can convey useful information, adding a subtle perspective.

comments.author: raw_anon_1111
comments.text: My thought process is that a diagram should stand on its own and should be understandable by non technical business people.  I always have callout notes as stickies on the diagram explaining what it does.
comments.analysis: This comment advocates for self-explanatory diagrams with annotations, which is a practical approach to improve clarity.

comments.author: layer8
comments.text:
> Meaningless animations

As someone who usually hates animations, in the example given I actually find them useful, assuming that they are representative of the actual flow. They are also unobtrusive because they are steady-state.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced view on the use of animations in diagrams, noting their potential usefulness if accurate.
